question: Will many open source advocates agree frontier models have crossed capability thresholds too dangerous to open source?
textDescription:
...within two years (e.g. by 6/1/26)?

This is Sam Hammond's Thesis IV/2 from https://www.secondbest.ca/p/ninety-five-theses-on-ai. 

I will consider many to mean at least 50%. 

If in my subjective judgment this is unclear I will ask at least one open source advocate for their view on how this resolves, and act accordingly. 

Resolves to YES if in my subjective judgment, at least half of those who in May 2024 were 'open source advocates,' weighted by prominence, agree that it would be too dangerous to release the weights of what are then SoTA frontier models. 

Resolves to NO if this is not true.
newsShortReason: Recent debates and expert opinions indicate a growing concern among open source advocates about the risks of releasing frontier AI models.
newsKeyEvidence: Lawrence Lessig, a prominent open-source advocate, expressed concerns about the risks associated with open-weight AI models, likening them to a 'bomb' that could be misused. ([transformernews.ai](https://www.transformernews.ai/p/lawrence-lessig-open-source-ai-risks?utm_source=openai)) | The OECD/GPAI report highlights that AI openness exists on a spectrum, with open-weight models revealing only a fraction of the information required for full accountability, suggesting that many open source advocates may agree on the dangers of releasing frontier models. ([oecd.ai](https://oecd.ai/en/wonk/balancing-innovation-transparency-and-risk-in-open-weight-models?utm_source=openai)) | A report by the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA) endorses open-source AI but acknowledges the risks of misuse, indicating that open source advocates are aware of the potential dangers. ([ccn.com](https://www.ccn.com/news/technology/open-source-ai-deepfake-dangers/?utm_source=openai))
